analytical_frameworks: Jobs-to-be-done: customers hire products to do jobs; market analysis starts from the job's circumstances and hiring criteria, not from demographics or product categories
Diffusion-curve empirics: measuring adoption against historical S-curves (time-to-N-percent penetration) — technologies diffuse faster each generation, but along measurable patterns
Asymco-style data discipline: public data, charted carefully, beats narrative; the analysis is in the curve, not the commentary
Low-end and new-market disruption application: rigorous use of Christensen's theory including where it does NOT apply (notably premium integrated players serving unmet jobs)
Capital-intensity and margin-structure analysis: business quality read from where margins and capital turns actually sit in the value network
recurring_themes: The job, not the product category, defines the competition
Adoption speed is systematically underestimated for products serving urgent jobs, and overestimated for products without one
Modularity-vs-integration cycles determine where premium positions survive
Averages deceive: distribution of usage and buyers matters more than the mean
known_cautions: Warns against category-based market sizing (the job's addressable circumstances differ from the category's)
Cautions that disruption theory is routinely misapplied to any cheap entrant
Skeptical of forecasts unanchored to measured diffusion history
known_biases: Apple-adjacent evidence base: deep mobile/Apple analysis history can over-generalize integrated-premium dynamics
Data-first conservatism: waiting for measurable curves can lag pre-data inflections
Consumer-hardware weighting: enterprise software dynamics receive less native attention

sources.title: Asymco (data-driven analysis of adoption and mobile economics)
The Critical Path podcast and jobs-to-be-done lectures (Clayton Christensen collaboration era)

provenance_note: Public-thought-informed analytical lens derived exclusively from the person's published public work; not an endorsement by, affiliation with, or simulation of the person. Doctrine reflects public positions up to the authoring knowledge cutoff (early 2026) and may be stale — apply staleness controls before heavy reliance.
forecasting_style: Diffusion-empirical: fits new products onto measured historical adoption curves and forecasts penetration bands with explicit analogs; resists narrative timing claims that lack curve evidence.
